You can indeed distinguish between server and CLI modes without external variables.
The Flask CLI automatically passes the app context to your command functions.
You might check the command name inside your CLI function to decide logic.
Another option is to inspect the `sys.argv` list within your command handler.
This allows you to detect if the script was called via the CLI interface.
You could also create a custom `create_app` that reads a specific flag.
Setting a flag in your configuration file is another viable approach for this.
The `app` object itself holds information about how it was invoked.
You can access the `cli` object to see which command is currently running.
This method avoids the need for manual environment variable setup every time.
Consider adding a boolean flag to your config that defaults to false.
Flip this flag to true only when the CLI command is explicitly detected.
Your `create_app` function can then check this flag before initializing.
This ensures your app behaves differently for server versus CLI execution.
You might also use the `flask run` command to trigger server mode logic.
The CLI commands will naturally trigger the alternative logic path you define.
No extra user input is required to switch between these two modes.
This solution keeps your workflow clean and efficient for daily tasks.

This approach maintains the flexibility of your current factory pattern design.
